content.text: What it is

A baseline current state audit is a time-boxed, evidence-based inventory and assessment of an organization's existing AI assets, data resources, policies, talent, and infrastructure. Its purpose is to produce a documented, timestamped baseline—the 'as-is' state—against which future maturity assessments can measure progress. Unlike a forward-looking strategy document, the baseline audit is strictly descriptive: it captures what exists today, at what level of maturity, with what gaps.

The audit produces a minimum viable artifact set: a model registry, a data asset catalog, a policy and control inventory, and a talent skill map. Together, these artifacts give the ESC a factual foundation for gap closure planning and investment decisions rather than relying on anecdote or self-reporting from individual teams.
Why it matters

AI governance discussions frequently suffer from an inventory gap: no one knows exactly how many AI models are deployed, where they run, who owns them, or what data they consume. This gap is not academic. In a regulatory audit, the inability to enumerate deployed AI systems is itself a compliance failure in jurisdictions that require an AI asset register. In operational terms, unknown models are unmanaged risk.

The baseline audit closes the inventory gap. It also reveals the delta between what program sponsors believe the organization has (often optimistic) and what actually exists. This delta is what makes the audit politically valuable: it converts subjective debates about AI readiness into empirically grounded ones.
Benefits
Creates the factual inventory required for regulatory compliance and internal governance
Establishes a timestamped baseline that makes future maturity improvement measurable
Surfaces unknown or shadow AI deployments that carry unmanaged risk
Provides the data needed for ROI analysis: what is already deployed and what value is it delivering
Aligns executive perception with operational reality, often revealing a maturity gap that motivates investment
Produces audit-ready artifacts that reduce preparation time when regulators inquire
Trade-offs and limitations
Shadow AI (models deployed outside sanctioned processes) is inherently hard to discover; the audit may undercount
Data quality of the resulting artifacts depends on cooperation from business unit owners who may resist disclosure
A point-in-time snapshot depreciates quickly in fast-moving AI environments; artifacts require quarterly refresh
The artifact set requires ongoing maintenance investment; producing it once without a maintenance plan creates a false sense of governance
Interview-based discovery misses systems that teams have forgotten about or do not recognize as AI
How it works

The audit runs in four sequential steps: inventory, classify, assess, and baseline. In the inventory step, the team collects a raw list of all AI-related assets—models, datasets, APIs, tools, and policies—across the organization. Sources include cloud billing records (AI API spend), IT asset management systems, interview-based disclosure forms, and code repository scans for ML framework imports.

In the classify step, each asset is assigned a type (predictive model, generative model, rules engine, etc.) and a risk tier based on the organization's risk framework—typically mapping business impact and autonomy level to low, medium, high, or critical. High and critical tier assets require additional audit depth: documented owner, production usage metrics, and evidence of review.

In the assess step, the collected artifact set is scored against the maturity dimension indicators defined in the dimensions assessment framework. The model registry's completeness scores governance maturity; the data catalog coverage scores data maturity; training completion rates score talent maturity. The baseline step records all scores, artifact versions, and assessment dates in the organization's governance document repository.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Current state audit workflow showing four steps: inventory, classify, assess, baseline, and the resulting artifact registry

content.text: The workflow produces four living artifacts maintained after the initial audit. The artifact registry is the single source of truth for AI governance reporting and forms the primary evidence package for regulatory inquiries and internal audits.
When to use it
Before the first formal maturity assessment, to have facts rather than estimates for indicator scoring
Before presenting an AI investment proposal to the board, to demonstrate a factual understanding of current state
When a new regulation (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, sector-specific rules) requires an AI asset register
After a major organizational restructuring that may have created orphaned or duplicated AI deployments
When a merger or acquisition brings an unknown AI estate into the organization
How to implement
Define the audit scope: which business units, geographies, and AI asset types are in scope; explicitly include or exclude third-party AI tools
Design the minimum viable artifact schemas: model registry, data catalog, policy inventory, and skill map field definitions
Run discovery: combine automated scanning (cloud spend analysis, code repos, IT asset management) with business unit disclosure forms
Conduct gap interviews: 45-minute structured sessions with the technology lead and the business lead for each unit to validate automated discovery
Classify and risk-tier all discovered assets; flag any high or critical risk assets outside sanctioned channels as shadow AI
Score maturity indicators using artifact completeness and quality data
Publish the baseline report to the ESC and establish a quarterly artifact refresh cadence with named owners for each artifact type
Examples

# Current State Audit — Minimum Viable Artifact Set

1. AI Model Registry (CSV or tool)
   Fields: model_id, name, purpose, business_unit, owner_name,
           deployment_status, model_type, vendor_or_internal,
           last_updated, risk_tier, audit_required (Y/N)

2. Data Asset Catalog (AI-relevant datasets)
   Fields: dataset_id, name, source_system, business_unit,
           contains_PII, data_quality_score, lineage_tracked (Y/N),
           access_controls, AI_use_cases_using_this

3. Policy and Control Inventory
   Fields: policy_id, name, status (draft/approved/superseded),
           owner, last_reviewed, applicable_AI_risk_categories,
           compliance_frameworks_mapped

4. Talent and Skill Map
   Fields: role_title, count, business_unit, AI_skills_required,
           current_proficiency (L1-L5), training_completion_rate

5. Infrastructure Inventory
   Fields: system_id, type (MLOps/data platform/LLM API),
           vendor, deployment_model (cloud/on-prem/hybrid),
           integration_points, SLA, cost_per_month

# Baseline scoring from audit artifacts

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ModelRegistryEntry:
    model_id: str
    name: str
    owner: str
    deployment_status: str      # dev / staging / production / deprecated
    risk_tier: str              # low / medium / high / critical
    audit_required: bool
    last_updated: date

def assess_model_governance_maturity(registry: list) -> int:
    # Returns L1-L3 based on observable registry completeness
    if not registry:
        return 1   # L1: no registry at all
    has_all_owners = all(r.owner for r in registry)
    has_risk_tiers = all(r.risk_tier for r in registry)
    production_audited = all(
        r.audit_required
        for r in registry
        if r.deployment_status == 'production'
        and r.risk_tier in ('high', 'critical')
    )
    if has_all_owners and has_risk_tiers and production_audited:
        return 3   # L3: systematic governance
    elif has_all_owners and has_risk_tiers:
        return 2   # L2: policy exists, coverage incomplete
    else:
        return 1   # L1: ad-hoc

# Baseline documentation — executive summary section

CURRENT STATE AI BASELINE — [Organization] — [Date]
Prepared by: [Assessment Team]
Scope: [Business units, geographies, AI asset types in scope]

ASSET SUMMARY
  Total AI models inventoried:         47
  In production (any environment):     23
  High/critical risk tier:             8
  Models with documented owners:       41 of 47 (87%)
  Models with active audit coverage:   6 of 8 required (75%)

DATA SUMMARY
  AI-relevant datasets cataloged:      112
  Datasets with quality scores:        68 of 112 (61%)
  Datasets with PII flags:             29
  Datasets with lineage tracked:       34 of 112 (30%)

POLICY SUMMARY
  AI policies required (by framework): 12
  Policies approved and in force:      5
  Policies in draft:                   4
  Policy gaps (not started):           3

OVERALL MATURITY SCORES BY DIMENSION
  Strategy:    L3   Technology:  L3
  Data:        L2   Culture:     L2
  Governance:  L1   Talent:      L2
  Binding constraint: Governance (L1)
Real-world example

A European bank undertook a current state audit in preparation for EU AI Act compliance. The bank's technology team estimated 30 AI models in production before the audit. The audit—combining cloud billing analysis, code repository scans, and business unit disclosure forms—identified 94 distinct AI assets including 47 models, 23 AI-enhanced data pipelines, and 24 AI-assisted decision tools embedded in vendor software.

Twelve of the 47 models were classified as high-risk under the EU AI Act's definition. Of these, only three had documented human oversight mechanisms as required. The audit findings directly drove a compliance remediation program and a $1.2M investment in model documentation and oversight tooling.
Common mistakes
Limiting discovery to IT-sanctioned systems and missing shadow AI deployed by business units using no-code tools or direct API access
Treating the baseline as a one-time artifact rather than a living document with a refresh cadence
Defining scope too broadly and running out of time before completing the inventory—a scoped but complete audit is better than a partial enterprise one
Failing to assign owners for each artifact type after the audit; ownerless artifacts are not refreshed
Conflating the asset inventory with the maturity assessment—inventory comes first and is a factual task; assessment involves judgment
Not presenting shadow AI findings to the ESC, leaving the risk unmanaged and the ESC poorly informed
Best practices
Start discovery with cloud billing: AI API spend in cost management tools provides a reliable and unbiased starting inventory
Use a standardized disclosure form for business units so data is in a consistent format before interviews begin
Classify risk tiers using a documented framework before scoring assets—post-hoc classification is disputed by asset owners
Flag but do not suppress shadow AI findings; bring them to the ESC with a proposed remediation path
Assign a named artifact owner for each inventory type who is accountable for quarterly refresh
Store the artifact set in a governance system with version history, not a shared drive where files get overwritten
Performance and scalability

For organizations with more than 50 AI assets, manual audit becomes impractical. At this scale, automated discovery tooling is necessary: cloud provider AI service inventories, SBOM scanning for ML framework dependencies, and API gateway logs showing calls to LLM endpoints. These tools reduce inventory time from weeks to days but produce raw data that still requires human classification and risk-tiering.

Quarterly refresh of the artifact registry should be automated where possible. Model registry updates trigger from the MLOps deployment pipeline; data catalog updates trigger from data platform metadata APIs; policy inventory updates are driven by the governance team's change management workflow.
Tooling and ecosystem
Model registries: MLflow, Weights & Biases Model Registry, Azure ML Model Registry, SageMaker Model Registry
Data catalogs: Alation, Collibra, Apache Atlas, dbt Cloud semantic layer, Databricks Unity Catalog
Policy management: ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or SharePoint with approval workflows
Cloud AI spend discovery: AWS Cost Explorer, Azure Cost Management, GCP Billing with AI service filters
Code scanning: GitHub Advanced Security, Semgrep, or custom scripts scanning for ML framework imports

Security and reliability

The baseline artifact set—especially the model registry and data catalog—contains sensitive organizational information including model architectures, data sources containing PII, and security control gaps. Store artifacts in access-controlled repositories with role-based permissions. The model registry and shadow AI findings in particular must not be broadly distributed.

Artifact version history is essential for reliability. When a regulator or auditor asks 'what was your AI asset inventory on date X?', the version-controlled repository must be able to answer that question. Systems that overwrite rather than version artifacts cannot support this requirement.
Key takeaways
The baseline audit produces the evidence base for all subsequent AI governance: model registry, data catalog, policy inventory, and skill map
Discovery must use multiple methods—automated scanning, cloud billing analysis, and business unit disclosure—to surface shadow AI
A timestamped, version-controlled baseline is the prerequisite for measuring maturity improvement over time
Shadow AI findings must be reported to the ESC, not suppressed; unmanaged risk does not disappear when it goes unreported
Artifact maintenance with named owners and quarterly refresh cadence transforms a one-time audit into ongoing governance infrastructure
